title: Concertina book with pockets
subtitle: An accordion-fold structure with glued-in pockets on alternating panels
excerpt: A concertina book folds back and forth in a continuous zigzag; adding pockets to alternate panels turns it into a display structure that holds photographs, cards, or folded notes. The whole thing is made from a single long strip of paper with no sewing.
sourceNotes: Esther K. Smith, How to Make Books (2007, Potter Craft), accordion and concertina chapter. The pocket variation follows a common extension taught in artist's book workshops.

content.text: book stands up on a table and shows both sides when fully open. That makes it well suited to photographs or an illustrated sequence. Adding pockets on every second panel doubles the display space. Items slip into the pockets rather than being glued down, so you can swap them out.

items.qty: One strip: 200 mm tall × (panel width × number of panels), e.g. for eight A6-wide panels: 200 mm × 840 mm
items.name: Cartridge paper, 220 gsm, for the concertina spine strip

items.qty: Four pieces, each 170 mm × panel width, the pockets fold into the concertina
items.name: Cartridge paper, 130 gsm, for the pocket panels

items.qty: Small quantity, in a dish with a brush
items.name: Acid-free PVA

items.qty: Two pieces cut to the same size as one panel
items.name: Cover boards, 1.5 mm (optional hard covers)

content.text: Score and fold the spine strip
Mark the panel divisions on the inside face of the strip: for A6-landscape panels, mark every 148 mm. Score lightly with the bone folder against a ruler. Fold the first mark as a
, the second as a mountain fold, and alternate through the strip. All folds must be firm and flat. Press the folded strip under a heavy book for 10 minutes before adding the pockets.

For each pocket panel, fold a 130 gsm sheet in half lengthwise. The folded strip is 85 mm tall by panel width wide. Apply a thin line of PVA along the bottom edge and both side edges of the folded strip. Press the pocket onto the outer face of a valley panel, aligning the bottom edge of the pocket with the panel bottom. Attach pockets to every second panel so that when the concertina closes, the pockets fold inward and lie flat.

Cut two pieces of 1.5 mm board to the panel dimensions. Glue a piece of decorative paper or plain book cloth over each board with turn-ins, as for a simple case. Glue the first panel of the concertina flat against the inside of the front board, and the last panel against the inside of the back board. Press under boards and weights for 30 minutes each.

Japanese stab-binding or Coptic-bound pamphlets can be glued as panels onto a concertina spine. That gives you a piece that works as both a book and a display object. You can also build a concertina from two shorter strips of paper joined with a hinge. Overlap the end panels by 3 mm, apply PVA and press, then fold back to check the join is flush before the glue sets.
